explain.rule: After some verbs, use a gerund (verb + -ing). After other verbs, use an infinitive (to + base verb). Some verbs can take both with a difference in meaning. Gerund (-ing): enjoy, finish, mind, avoid, consider, suggest, practise, miss, deny, imagine, keep, risk, can't help, look forward to, be used to. Infinitive (to + verb): want, need, decide, plan, hope, agree, refuse, manage, offer, promise, seem, tend, forget, remember (future), try (attempt). Both with different meanings: stop, remember, forget, try.
explain.why_matters: Gerunds and infinitives are everywhere in English. Getting this right is the difference between sounding natural ('I enjoy swimming') and sounding like a learner ('I enjoy to swim'). Many of the most common verbs in English require one form or the other.
explain.hebrew_note: Hebrew uses the infinitive (לשחות, לאכול) after most verbs, with very few requiring a different form. In English, the choice between -ing and to-infinitive depends entirely on the preceding verb — there is no logical rule, only lists to learn. The most common Hebrew speaker mistake is using 'to' infinitive after enjoy, finish, avoid, mind, consider — verbs that require -ing in English.
explain.tip: Learn these key verbs by heart — they always take -ing: enjoy, finish, mind, avoid, keep, practise, miss, consider, imagine, suggest, deny, risk. These always take to-infinitive: want, need, decide, hope, plan, agree, refuse, manage, offer, expect, seem. For the verbs that take both, meaning changes: 'I stopped to smoke' (I stopped what I was doing in order to smoke) vs 'I stopped smoking' (I quit the habit).

meaning_changes.infinitive: stop to smoke = pause in order to smoke

meaning_changes.gerund: remember meeting her = I have a memory of meeting her
meaning_changes.infinitive: remember to call = don't forget to call (future task)

meaning_changes.gerund: forget meeting him = I have no memory of it
meaning_changes.infinitive: forget to lock = I didn't lock because I forgot (future task)

meaning_changes.gerund: try eating less sugar = experiment with it
meaning_changes.infinitive: try to sleep = make an effort to sleep

meaning_changes.gerund: regret saying that = sorry I said it
meaning_changes.infinitive: regret to inform = formally sorry to tell you something

good_examples.note: enjoy always takes -ing. Never 'enjoy to read'.

good_examples.sentence: She decided to change her career.
good_examples.audio_text: She decided to change her career.

good_examples.sentence: Have you finished writing the report?
good_examples.audio_text: Have you finished writing the report?

good_examples.sentence: I'm looking forward to meeting you.
good_examples.audio_text: I'm looking forward to meeting you.
good_examples.note: look forward to + -ing (to is a preposition here, not part of infinitive).

good_examples.sentence: He refused to sign the contract.
good_examples.audio_text: He refused to sign the contract.

good_examples.sentence: I stopped smoking five years ago.
good_examples.audio_text: I stopped smoking five years ago.

good_examples.sentence: I stopped to have a coffee on the way.
good_examples.audio_text: I stopped to have a coffee on the way.
good_examples.note: stopped to have = paused in order to (infinitive)

good_examples.sentence: She managed to get tickets for the concert.
good_examples.audio_text: She managed to get tickets for the concert.

good_examples.sentence: Would you mind closing the window?
good_examples.audio_text: Would you mind closing the window?

good_examples.note: remember + to-infinitive = don't forget to do something future.

bad_examples.reason: enjoy always takes -ing. Never 'enjoy to swim'.
avoid always takes -ing. Never 'avoid to go'.

bad_examples.wrong: He suggested to take a taxi.
bad_examples.reason: suggest always takes -ing. Never 'suggest to take'.

bad_examples.wrong: I'm looking forward to meet you.
bad_examples.correct: I'm looking forward to meeting you.
bad_examples.reason: 'Look forward to' — the 'to' here is a preposition, not part of an infinitive. After 'to' (preposition) → -ing.

bad_examples.wrong: Would you mind to open the door?
bad_examples.correct: Would you mind opening the door?
bad_examples.reason: mind always takes -ing. Never 'mind to open'.

exercises.question: I enjoy ___ (cook) when I have time.
exercises.explanation: enjoy always takes -ing: enjoy cooking.

exercises.question: She decided ___ (change) her job.
exercises.explanation: decide always takes to: decided to change.

exercises.question: Have you finished ___ (write) the report?
exercises.explanation: finish always takes -ing: finished writing.

exercises.question: I'm looking forward to ___ (see) you again.
exercises.explanation: look forward to + -ing. Not 'to see'.

exercises.question: He refused ___ (sign) the contract.
exercises.explanation: refuse takes to: refused to sign.

exercises.question: Would you mind ___ (turn) down the music?
exercises.explanation: mind takes -ing: mind turning.

exercises.question: She managed ___ (get) tickets for the show.
exercises.explanation: manage takes to: managed to get.

exercises.question: I avoid ___ (drive) in rush hour if I can.
exercises.explanation: avoid takes -ing: avoid driving.

exercises.question: Please remember ___ (call) me tomorrow morning.
exercises.hint: remember + to-infinitive = don't forget to do it (future)
exercises.explanation: remember to call = don't forget to call (future task).

exercises.question: I remember ___ (meet) her at a conference last year.
exercises.hint: remember + -ing = I have a memory of it (past)
exercises.explanation: remember meeting = I have a memory of it.

exercises.question: She stopped ___ (smoke) after the doctor's advice.
exercises.explanation: stopped smoking = gave up the habit.

exercises.question: He stopped ___ (get) some water on the way.
exercises.hint: stopped + to-infinitive = paused in order to
exercises.explanation: stopped to get = paused what he was doing in order to get water.

exercises.question: I can't imagine ___ (live) without my phone.
exercises.explanation: imagine takes -ing: imagine living.

exercises.question: They suggested ___ (book) in advance.
exercises.explanation: suggest takes -ing: suggest booking.

exercises.question: I enjoy ___ to new places.
exercises.explanation: enjoy always takes -ing.

exercises.question: She hopes ___ a place at medical school.
exercises.explanation: hope always takes to: hope to get.

exercises.question: I'm used to ___ up early.
exercises.explanation: be used to + -ing. to is a preposition here, not part of infinitive.

exercises.question: He denied ___ the money.
exercises.explanation: deny always takes -ing: denied taking.

exercises.question: We agreed ___ the meeting until Thursday.
exercises.explanation: agree takes to: agreed to postpone.

exercises.question: Would you consider ___ abroad for a few years?
exercises.explanation: consider takes -ing: consider working.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'I enjoy to swim in the sea.'
exercises.answer: I enjoy swimming in the sea.
exercises.explanation: enjoy always takes -ing. enjoy swimming.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'She avoided to go there alone.'
exercises.explanation: avoid takes -ing. avoided going.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'I'm looking forward to meet you.'
exercises.answer: I'm looking forward to meeting you.
exercises.hint: Look forward to — what kind of 'to' is this?
exercises.explanation: to in 'look forward to' is a preposition → -ing follows. meeting.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.explanation: I really enjoy learning new languages and meeting new people.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.explanation: Remember to send the report before you leave the office.

speak.sentence: I really enjoy exploring new cities and trying local food.
chunks: I really enjoy exploring new cities

speak.sentence: She decided to leave her job and start her own business.
chunks: She decided to leave her job

speak.sentence: Would you mind closing the window? It's a bit cold in here.
chunks: Would you mind closing the window?
It's a bit cold in here.

speak.sentence: I'm really looking forward to seeing everyone at the reunion.
chunks: I'm really looking forward to seeing everyone

speak.sentence: He managed to finish all the work before the deadline.
chunks: He managed to finish all the work

speak.sentence: Remember to take your passport — you'll need it at check-in.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Passport' = PAS-port — stress on first syllable. 'Check-in' = CHEK-in.

speak.sentence: I stopped eating meat about two years ago.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Eating' = EE-ting. stopped + eating = gave up the habit.

speak.sentence: She avoided making any promises until she had all the information.
chunks: until she had all the information.
